items.q: What property do shape memory alloys have?
items.correct: They return to a pre-set shape when heated.
items.misconception: They remember every shape they have ever been bent into.
items.why: Students think SMAs are infinitely programmable. They actually have ONE 'remembered' shape, set during high-temperature treatment. Heating restores that shape; the alloy doesn't store a history of deformations.

items.q: What is the difference between ductility and malleability?
items.correct: Ductility is being drawn into wires; malleability is being hammered into sheets.
items.misconception: They mean exactly the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Ductility is for metals, malleability is for plastics.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms because both involve deformation. Ductility = tensile (stretching into wires); malleability = compressive (rolling/hammering into sheets). Some metals excel at one but not the other.

items.q: What is the difference between CAD and CAM?
items.correct: CAD creates digital designs; CAM uses those designs to control manufacturing machines.
items.misconception: They are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: CAD is for cars, CAM is for cameras.
items.why: Students treat the terms as synonymous because they often appear together. CAD = drawing/modelling software; CAM = machine control (CNC routers, 3D printers) - typically the second stage.

items.q: Which is an example of a hardwood?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Oak. “Pine” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Medium-Density Fibreboard. “Multi-Density Fibreboard” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the typical maximum moisture content (percentage) of seasoned timber for indoor furniture?
items.why: Exact answer: 12 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
